metadata.title:
SPOKANE DEEP
IN bALL|NGER


P|NCH0T CASE

THIS CITY HAS FIGURED IN
CONTROVERSY FROM THE


BEGINNING

pages.raw_text:
Spokane probably has more
reason to be interested in the Bal
linger-Pinchot investigation now in
progress in Washington than any
other city in the country. The in
vestigation was precipitated by the
national irrigation congress in this
city last August, when Pinchot and
his friends first launched the attack
on Ballinger. From that time for
ward the controversy has grown I'M
til it has involved the whole country
in a scandal that threatens to wreck
the Taft administration.


The chief factor in precipitating
the Ballinger-Pinchot controversy
was the effort of the claimants to
the Cunningham coal lands in
Alaska to have their claims pat
ented by the government. These
coal lands are largely a Spokane
proposition, involving a number of
prominent Spokane citizens, and
people living in the Inland Empire
The two meetings of the Cunning
hi m coal land claimants, in which it
iasalieged they tried to circumvent
tt rules of the land office in the
premature sale of their claims to
the Guggenheim syndicate, were
held In Spokane.


Horace T. Jones, who testified
Friday in the Ballinger-Pinchot in
vestigation; Lofts R. CLAWS, who
preceded him on the stand, and
James M. Sheridan, the government
attorney whose ability as lawyer
was assailed by these two wit
nesses, were present in Spokane
last December during the Cunning
ham coal lands hearing. Sheridan
was the attorney in charge of the
government examination of the
claimants and it was burning ques-
ton while he was here whether or
not he was making an effort to get
at the bottom of the facts in the
case. Sheridan said he was, but
CLAWS and Jones have testified
that he was not.
